title: Non-uniqueness of Leray solutions of the forced Navier-Stokes equations
speaker: Maria Colombo
abstract: In his seminal work, Leray demonstrated the existence of global weak solutions, with nonincreasing energy, to the Navier-Stokes equations in three dimensions. In this talk we exhibit two distinct Leray solutions with zero initial velocity and identical body force.
tags: Workshop on Recent developments in incompressible fluid dynamics

proof_stages.label: Weak-solution uniqueness problem and forced theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames uniqueness for Navier-Stokes and Euler, reviews Leray and suitable weak solutions, recalls what convex integration gives outside the Leray class, and states the target result: nonuniqueness of forced three-dimensional Leray solutions with zero initial data and force in the natural Leray existence class.

proof_stages.label: Two-dimensional Euler analogues and motivations
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the Yudovich bounded-vorticity uniqueness theorem, the larger low-vorticity existence classes, convex-integration and numerical attempts at nonuniqueness, and Vishik's forced two-dimensional Euler result as a model for the forced Navier-Stokes strategy.

proof_stages.label: Self-similar variables and perturbation equation
proof_stages.summary: Navier-Stokes is rewritten in similarity variables, sending the physical initial time to τ=-∞, and a background self-similar profile is perturbed so that a nonzero perturbation vanishing as τ→-∞ would yield a second solution with the same initial data and force.

proof_stages.label: Unstable eigenmode and nonlinear unstable manifold
proof_stages.summary: The perturbation equation is split into a linearized self-similar Navier-Stokes operator and a quadratic remainder; an unstable eigenvalue gives an exponentially decaying mode backward in self-similar time, and a fixed-point argument corrects it to a genuine nonlinear solution.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of the spectral problem to Euler
proof_stages.summary: Because instability of a generic self-similar Navier-Stokes linearization is hard to prove, the background is multiplied by a large factor so that the Euler linearization dominates the viscous and scaling terms, allowing spectral perturbation to transfer Euler instability back to Navier-Stokes.

proof_stages.label: Unstable compactly supported two-dimensional vortex
proof_stages.summary: Using stability theory for two-dimensional Euler vortices and Vishik's nonmonotone-vorticity construction, the argument obtains a compactly supported two-dimensional vortex profile with an unstable eigenvalue.

proof_stages.label: Transfer to a large three-dimensional axisymmetric torus
proof_stages.summary: The compact two-dimensional vortex is placed far from the axis as a large axisymmetric no-swirl torus; as the torus radius tends to infinity the axisymmetric Euler equations converge to the two-dimensional Euler equations, so spectral perturbation transfers the unstable eigenvalue to the three-dimensional background, after correcting divergence errors.

examples.name: Resonance-chain desingularizations of homogeneous 2D Euler data
examples.note: The numerical proposal compares two ε-regularizations of the same singular self-similar vorticity, producing either one rotating vortex or two separated vortices; it is used as a picture of possible nonuniqueness, not as an ingredient in the Navier-Stokes proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes the homogeneous 2D Euler datum and explicitly runs two epsilon-desingularizations of it, comparing the resulting one-vortex versus two-vortex evolutions.

passes.reason: A scale-homogeneous singular-limit/desingularized Euler construction is used as a numerical candidate for two different evolutions from the same data.
Scale-homogeneous singular Euler data with an epsilon desingularization is a limiting/critical ansatz used as a prospective nonuniqueness counterexample.
Scale-homogeneous self-similar desingularizations approach a singular limiting profile and are offered as putative nonunique Euler limits.
Scale-homogeneous singular Euler data and their ε→0 desingularizations are limiting self-similar profiles used as a proposed numerical nonuniqueness mechanism.
Used as a numerical/candidate mechanism for nonuniqueness of 2D Euler from two desingularizations of the same homogeneous self-similar data.
a scaling-homogeneous singular Euler datum is desingularized by shrinking-core limits that are presented as candidate nonunique limits, so it is symmetric/limiting and adversarial to uniqueness
Used as a numerical candidate for failure of 2D Euler uniqueness via epsilon-to-zero desingularizations of a singular self-similar homogeneous datum.
Used as a numerical adversarial scenario for nonuniqueness of 2D Euler with the same singular homogeneous datum, and the desingularized profiles are scaling/limit profiles at a singular core.
scale-homogeneous desingularized Euler data are used as a proposed nonuniqueness mechanism.
used as a numerical candidate for nonuniqueness of 2D Euler, built from scale-homogeneous singular data via a zero-core limit
Used as a proposed numerical route to nonuniqueness for 2D Euler, based on scale-homogeneous singular data approached by desingularizing cutoffs.
It is a singular scale-invariant/desingularization-limit Euler scenario proposed to produce two limits from the same data, hence an adversarial nonuniqueness example.

examples.name: Shear flows as special profiles with known stability theory
examples.note: Shear flows are mentioned as a familiar special class where linearized stability is better understood, illustrating why a generic background-profile argument is unavailable; the proof then proceeds with vortices instead.
examples.engagement_reason: Shear flows are only named as special profiles with existing stability theory before the speaker says they will instead focus on vortices.

passes.reason: Shear flows are reduced one-dimensional, translation-invariant profiles invoked only as tractable stability cases.
Shear flows are reduced one-dimensional parallel-flow profiles invoked as tractable stability models, not to refute a claim.
Shear flows are highly ordered translation-invariant profiles cited as tractable stability models, not as failures of a claim.
Shear flows are translation-invariant one-dimensional flow profiles cited only as tractable backgrounds with known stability theory.
Shear flows are highly structured translation-invariant profiles cited as tractable stability cases, not as failures of a claim.
shear flows are translation-invariant, effectively one-dimensional unidirectional profiles with vanishing self-advection, cited only as tractable stability backgrounds
Cited only as a well-understood stability class, with shear profiles structurally distinguished by strong translational one-dimensional symmetry.
Shear flows are cited as special reference profiles where stability theory is understood, and structurally they reduce the flow to a highly constrained one-dimensional invariant profile.
shear flows are translation-invariant special profiles cited only as tractable stability examples.
cited as highly symmetric one-dimensional base profiles with tractable stability theory, not as a refutation
Cited only as tractable stability profiles, with parallel one-dimensional form and streamwise symmetry unlike generic flows.
Shear flows are lower-dimensional translation-invariant profiles used as tractable stability backgrounds rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes as a neighboring problem
examples.note: In Q&A the speaker mentions two-dimensional fractional Navier-Stokes with dissipation exponent below the critical one as another setting where an Euler instability can dominate a perturbative dissipative term.
examples.engagement_reason: Supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes is only cited in the Q&A as a related result where stability gives nonuniqueness in the supercritical range, without developing the example.

passes.reason: The supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes regime is a systematic neighboring family cited for related nonuniqueness, not a sporadic or endpoint object.
The supercritical fractional Navier–Stokes family is a systematic neighboring PDE regime used to exhibit failure of uniqueness.
The supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes family is cited as a systematic neighboring setting where nonuniqueness can be constructed.
The fractional Navier–Stokes model is chosen in the supercritical scaling regime and is mentioned as a neighboring setting where nonuniqueness can be built.
Supercritical fractional Navier–Stokes is mentioned as an adjacent model where the method extends, not as a specific adversarial example here.
supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes is a standard neighboring family, mentioned as a setting where the method yields nonuniqueness and hence failure of uniqueness
Mentioned as an adjacent equation for the method, while the broad supercritical fractional-dissipation regime is not a sporadic or extremal object.

Supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes is mentioned as a neighboring problem where the method extends, not as the main refuting example, and it is an ordinary member of a parameter family rather than a sporadic or boundary case.
the supercritical fractional Navier-Stokes problem is cited for a nonuniqueness result, not as a structural exception.
cited as a nearby setting where the method gives nonuniqueness throughout the supercritical side of the scaling threshold
Mentioned as an adjacent fractional-dissipation setting where the same instability method yields nonuniqueness in the supercritical range.
The supercritical fractional-dissipation model is invoked as a neighboring setting where the method yields nonuniqueness, with supercriticality placing it beyond the scaling threshold.
